topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hardware-aware, closed-loop quantum error correction protocol that integrates adaptive pulse control with real-time syndrome extraction and machine learning-driven noise prediction to dynamically optimize error correction cycles on semiconductor spin qubits in NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction protocols in NISQ devices suffer from static pulse sequences that cannot adapt to fast fluctuating charge noise and hardware instabilities, causing significant decoherence and limiting logical qubit lifetimes. Moreover, existing low-overhead QEC codes and compiler optimizations lack integrated real-time hardware feedback, resulting in suboptimal error suppression and limited coherence improvements. The open problem is how to co-design error correction cycles and adaptive control pulses that respond dynamically to real hardware noise, reducing logical error rates without excessive qubit overhead.

APPROACH:  
We propose a closed-loop QEC framework combining hardware-aware adaptive pulse control with in situ syndrome measurements and a machine learning (ML) model trained to predict near-term noise fluctuations from noisy hardware telemetry. This method uses real-time syndrome extraction to inform pulse parameter adjustments every QEC cycle, dynamically mitigating decoherence sources such as charge noise and slow gate drifts. The ML predictor guides pulse adaptation beyond classical feedback, enabling preemptive error suppression. This is implemented and validated on semiconductor spin qubits with integrated hardware feedback channels, combining adaptive optimized pulses (building on the winning approach) with QEC codes that allow fast syndrome extraction and correction.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the Quantum Device Noise Dataset (QDN-D), IBM Qiskit Noise Models, and the Google Sycamore error benchmarking dataset. Baselines include the static pulse QEC approach from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), the adaptive pulse control method from “Hardware-aware adaptive pulse control for decoherence mitigation in spin qubits” (2027), and surface code implementations with compiler optimizations from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026). Metrics include logical qubit coherence time extension (>20% improvement), reduction in logical error rate (target <0.005), and syndrome extraction latency (<1 µs overhead). Experiments will run on simulated spin qubit platforms with noise traces from hardware telemetry and on IBM Quantum Experience devices with repeated runs across 10 random seeds for statistical significance. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of ML prediction and pulse adaptation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that either use static QEC codes or open-loop pulse adaptation, this work is the first to integrate real-time QEC syndrome extraction with adaptive, ML-augmented pulse control in a closed feedback loop at the hardware level. This enables dynamic, preemptive error suppression tuned to instantaneous noise, surpassing static or purely reactive pulse schemes and reducing logical errors without requiring larger qubit overhead or complex fault-tolerant architectures.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an integrated error-aware quantum circuit compression framework that simultaneously minimizes gate count and optimizes adaptive low-overhead quantum error correction tailored for NISQ devices, validated on real quantum benchmark circuits under realistic noise models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NISQ devices suffer from limited qubit counts, high gate error rates, and restricted coherence times, making conventional quantum error correction (QEC) schemes impractical due to large overheads and complexity. Existing circuit compression methods either ignore error correction or add incompatible overhead, failing to achieve low logical error rates within the tight resource and noise constraints of NISQ hardware. The open problem is how to co-optimize circuit compression and error correction primitives to reduce gate counts and maintain or improve logical error rates on near-term quantum devices without exceeding their hardware limitations.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel error-aware quantum circuit compression algorithm that jointly optimizes gate-level reductions and embeds adaptive, low-overhead QEC codes customized per circuit segment based on local noise characteristics. The method uses a noise-profile-driven compiler backend that selectively inserts compact QEC primitives—such as tailored versions of Bacon-Shor or small-distance LDPC codes—combined with aggressive gate folding and gate cancellation techniques, to minimize both physical gate count and logical error rate under realistic NISQ noise models. The approach includes iterative noise-adaptive recompilation using qubit-specific error rates and coherence statistics to dynamically balance compression and error correction overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on benchmark quantum circuits from the IBM Quantum Experience (e.g., Quantum Volume circuits), Rigetti’s Aspen suite, and the QASMBench benchmark set, using synthetic yet empirically calibrated noise models (depolarizing, amplitude damping, and cross-talk). Compare against baselines: Gate count minimized error-aware circuit compression (the tournament winner’s method), Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices (2026), and Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design (2026). Metrics will include total physical gate count (target >30% reduction over baselines), logical error rate (maintain or reduce by at least 10%), and compilation latency (under 2x the baseline compilation time). Experiments will run on simulated hardware with 5 independent random seeds and include ablation studies on noise adaptation and QEC code selection.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, which treats circuit compression and error correction as separate or sequential tasks, this method co-optimizes both in a noise-adaptive, integrated manner specifically for NISQ constraints. It uniquely tailors QEC primitives dynamically within compressed circuits based on local noise profiles, enabling greater gate count reduction and improved logical error resilience simultaneously—achieving a balance unattainable by standalone compression or QEC approaches demonstrated in existing literature.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop and experimentally validate a family of scalable noise-adaptive quantum error correction codes tailored to NISQ devices that dynamically optimize detection and correction of correlated multi-qubit errors with minimal qubit overhead and real-time calibration.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) schemes, including surface codes and recently proposed low-overhead codes, do not effectively address the complex, correlated noise patterns and crosstalk prevalent in dense NISQ device architectures. This results in elevated logical error rates and excessive calibration demands, limiting both device scalability and practical usability for near-term quantum applications. There is a critical need for QEC codes that adaptively and efficiently handle these correlated errors while respecting the stringent resource constraints of NISQ-era hardware.

APPROACH: We propose a framework for noise-adaptive QEC codes that leverage real-time noise characterization to dynamically reconfigure stabilizer measurement protocols and syndrome decoding tailored to correlated error clusters detected in the hardware. The method integrates scalable tensor network-based decoders capable of efficiently processing correlated error syndromes with minimal classical overhead, and introduces a hierarchical calibration scheme that reduces overhead by focusing on noise hotspots identified via in situ error diagnostics. The codes themselves are constructed as modular concatenations of tailored block codes designed to target identified correlated noise structures, allowing scaling to larger qubit arrays typical of NISQ processors.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate the approach on benchmark datasets of experimentally gathered noise profiles from IBM Quantum Falcon r5.11 and Rigetti Aspen-11 devices, incorporating their publicly available noise tomography data. Baselines include the standard Surface Code (distance 3 and 5), the Low-Overhead QEC Code (10-qubit framework from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” 2026), and Noise-Adaptive Decoders from “Scalable Noise-Adaptive Codes for Large-Scale Quantum Data Centers.” Key metrics are logical error rate reduction (targeting >40% improvement over baseline low-overhead codes), qubit overhead reduction (aiming for at least 30% fewer qubits to reach logical error rate <0.01), and calibration time savings (at least 25% reduction vs baseline protocols). Experiments will use realistic noisy circuit simulations run on Qiskit Aer and Pennylane, with at least 5 random seeds per configuration plus ablation studies isolating the impact of adaptive decoding, modular code design, and calibration strategies.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either focuses on large-scale quantum data centers or static low-overhead codes for NISQ without real-time noise adaptation, this proposal uniquely integrates modular code design with scalable adaptive decoding and a hierarchical calibration reduction methodology specifically tuned for the correlated noise and limited qubit resources of NISQ devices—enabling practical logical error suppression not achievable by existing QEC schemes in near-term hardware.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid lightweight quantum error correction and mitigation protocol optimized for embedded quantum sensors on NISQ devices that dynamically adapts to hardware noise profiles with minimal qubit and classical overhead.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) codes designed for NISQ devices either require excessive qubit overhead (e.g., surface codes) or rely on static noise assumptions that do not hold in embedded quantum sensor environments with severe resource constraints. Embedded quantum sensors demand error suppression methods that incur minimal qubit and classical processing costs while maintaining robustness against time-varying noise and decoherence typical in real-world edge deployments.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid quantum-classical error mitigation framework that integrates a lightweight, adaptive QEC code tailored for low-qubit count embedded sensors with a real-time noise characterization module. This module uses fast classical feedback to adjust error correction parameters on-the-fly, leveraging a limited qubit budget (≤10 physical qubits). The approach combines low-overhead stabilizer codes inspired by the 10-qubit codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices” with a noise-adaptive compiler optimization layer derived from “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design for Efficient Qubit Mapping.” The system runs on near-term embedded hardware with constrained classical compute, employing hybrid quantum-classical control loops for practical in situ deployment.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on IBM Quantum Experience’s IBMQ Lima and Rigetti Aspen-9 devices using the Quantum Error Correction Benchmark Dataset (QECBD), the Quantum Device Noise Characterization Dataset (QDNCD), and the Embedded Sensor Data Set (ESDS) from recent quantum sensor experiments. Baselines include the standard Surface Code, the 10-qubit Low-Overhead QEC code from Gao et al. (2026), and the noise-aware quantum compiler from Li et al. (2026). Metrics will measure logical error rate reduction (targeting ≤0.004 logical error rate), qubit overhead (≤10 physical qubits), and latency (sub-100 microseconds error correction cycle time). Experiments will run over 50 random seeds with ablation studies on the noise adaptation module’s contribution.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that focus on either low-overhead QEC or compiler-level noise mitigation separately, our method uniquely fuses both with a dynamic, real-time noise characterization and adaptation loop specifically designed for the extreme constraints of embedded quantum sensors. This enables practical error correction in devices where traditional QEC codes and static mitigation techniques fail due to qubit and classical processing limitations.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a scalable, adaptive quantum error correction framework for NISQ devices that integrates real-time correlated noise diagnostics with low-overhead, dynamic code selection to reduce logical error rates while maintaining feasible classical processing requirements.

BACKGROUND: Existing adaptive quantum error correction (QEC) methods fail to efficiently manage correlated noise in NISQ devices due to prohibitive classical overhead and qubit overhead constraints. Current low-overhead QEC codes either do not address correlated multi-qubit errors effectively or require static code configurations that cannot adapt to time-varying noise profiles. Thus, there is an urgent need for scalable QEC frameworks tailored for NISQ architectures that dynamically mitigate correlated errors without incurring excessive classical computational costs.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid classical-quantum control system that continuously collects noise diagnostics from benchmark datasets such as IBM Quantum Experience calibration data, Rigetti Quantum Cloud Services noise profiles, and Google Sycamore device logs. Using these inputs, a lightweight machine learning model (e.g., a small recurrent neural network) runs on classical co-processors to identify dominant correlated error modes in real time. Based on this, the system dynamically selects from a family of parameterized, low-overhead QEC codes—including tailored variants of the surface code, Bacon-Shor codes, and recently proposed LDPC codes—optimized for correlated noise suppression under NISQ constraints. The method incorporates adaptive syndrome extraction scheduling to minimize classical processing latency and qubit overhead. Ablation studies will examine trade-offs between error suppression, code switching frequency, and classical computational cost.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on three benchmark noise datasets: IBM Quantum Experience calibration logs (2023), Rigetti Aspen noise profiles (2023), and Google Sycamore experimental noise data (2022). Baselines include standard surface code QEC, low-overhead Bacon-Shor codes (2026), and fixed-parameter LDPC codes (2026). Metrics include logical error rate reduction (target >40% improvement vs fixed-code baselines), classical processing latency (target <10 ms per QEC cycle), and qubit overhead (target <20% increase over low-overhead codes). Experiments will run on a simulated NISQ environment with noise injection, using 5 random seeds for statistical significance and include ablation on adaptive code selection and syndrome scheduling components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior NISQ-focused QEC work that either fixes codes or ignores correlated noise complexity, our approach simultaneously addresses time-varying correlated errors with adaptive, lightweight classical control tailored for scalable deployment. This enables practical real-time QEC in NISQ devices with complex noise environments without the prohibitive overhead that limits current adaptive QEC approaches in large-scale processors.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and validate physics-inspired scaling laws for large language models explicitly accounting for strict memory, power, and compute constraints of edge devices, enabling accurate prediction and principled optimization of LLM performance under real-world hardware bottlenecks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models assume unbounded growth in model size and compute, which fails to capture critical performance phase transitions and energy efficiency trade-offs on resource-constrained edge hardware platforms such as smartphones, IoT devices, and embedded systems. This gap prevents reliable extrapolation of LLM capabilities to edge deployment scenarios where strict memory, power, and thermal limits impose nonlinear bottlenecks on achievable model size and throughput.

APPROACH:  
We propose a new class of scaling laws that incorporate explicit hardware constraints modeled as energy landscape interactions and memory saturation effects inspired by statistical physics and thermodynamics. This framework integrates compute-memory-power budgets as key parameters, modeling performance as a function of not only model and dataset scale but also device-specific resource limits. We will derive analytic forms and validate these laws empirically by training and evaluating LLM variants under controlled edge-like constraints, using hardware-in-the-loop simulation to capture real power and memory bottlenecks.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on natural language benchmarks SQuAD 2.0, SuperGLUE, and WikiText-103, using GPT-2 Small, GPT-2 Medium, and DistilBERT as baselines. Measure perplexity (target < 20 on WikiText-103), exact match and F1 score improvements (>2% absolute on SQuAD 2.0), and inference latency reductions (>30% on edge hardware simulators). Experimental protocol includes training with a fixed compute budget under simulated memory and power caps reflective of edge devices (e.g., 2-4GB RAM, <5W TDP), running ablations across model sizes and quantization schemes with at least 5 random seeds to assess robustness.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws that only address unconstrained or ideal compute regimes, our model explicitly incorporates physical hardware constraints and their nonlinear impacts on efficiency and accuracy, providing the first predictive framework tailored for edge LLM deployments. This enables principled design decisions inaccessible to existing inverse problem or statistical physics-inspired approaches that do not model device-level bottlenecks.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified energy-aware scaling law framework that jointly optimizes model size, dataset size, and training schedule across heterogeneous hardware resources to minimize both predictive error and total energy consumption during large language model training.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) assume homogeneous compute environments and focus solely on minimizing prediction error or perplexity, ignoring the heterogeneous energy costs and communication overheads characteristic of real-world distributed data centers. This mismatch results in inefficient scaling regimes that underutilize mixed hardware accelerators and lead to excessive energy use and training costs. The open problem is how to formulate and empirically validate scaling laws that explicitly incorporate heterogeneous compute energy profiles and network communication costs into the optimization of LLM training efficiency.

APPROACH:  
We propose an energy-based scaling optimization framework that models compute energy consumption, communication overhead, and hardware heterogeneity as an integrated energy functional. This functional is incorporated into a joint optimization problem that balances model size, dataset size, and training compute budget to minimize a composite loss combining prediction error and total system energy. The method leverages empirical energy profiling of heterogeneous accelerators (GPUs, TPUs, custom ASICs) and network topology to derive globally optimal scaling regimes. We implement and validate this framework via distributed training experiments with mixed hardware, dynamically adjusting training schedules to minimize energy-delay-product while meeting target perplexity thresholds.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three widely used language modeling benchmarks: WikiText-103, The Pile, and C4. Baselines include standard scaling laws-based schedules from Kaplan et al. (2020), inverse-problem scaling laws from the 2025 position paper, and homogeneous-environment optimized runs using GPT-4-sized architectures. Metrics include perplexity (target < 15 on WikiText-103), total training energy consumption (aiming for >20% reduction compared to homogeneous baseline), and wall-clock training time (reduce by >15%). Experiments will be conducted on a heterogeneous hardware cluster composed of NVIDIA A100 GPUs, Google TPU v4, and custom AI chips with measured per-step energy costs. We will run each configuration with 3 seeds and perform ablations isolating the impact of communication cost modeling and hardware heterogeneity inclusion.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws that ignore heterogeneous hardware energy costs or assume homogeneous compute, our method explicitly integrates real-world energy and communication cost models into the scaling law formulation, enabling globally optimal training schedules that reduce energy consumption and training time in mixed-hardware environments. It goes beyond inverse-problem and purely statistical scaling approaches by embedding system-level constraints and energy-aware optimization directly into the scaling law framework.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop empirically validated scaling laws for multilingual large language models that explicitly incorporate data scarcity, linguistic diversity, and cross-lingual transfer dynamics to optimize model size and data allocation for underrepresented and low-resource languages.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing scaling laws for large language models largely assume abundant and balanced training data, failing to account for the realities of multilingual settings where many languages have scarce and highly imbalanced datasets. This limits understanding of how model performance scales across languages with varying resource levels and linguistic characteristics. Without such laws, it is unclear how to best allocate compute and data resources to maximize performance on low-resource languages while maintaining overall multilingual capability.

APPROACH:  
We propose to extend classical scaling law methodologies by integrating novel metrics quantifying data quality, linguistic diversity (e.g., typological features, language family distances), and cross-lingual transfer effectiveness into the scaling function. Using a controlled set of multilingual datasets spanning high-, mid-, and low-resource languages, we will empirically model how these factors influence scaling exponents for loss and downstream task performance. This includes formulating a joint scaling law that predicts performance as a function of model size, dataset size per language, and linguistic relatedness, enabling principled guidance on optimal data/model scaling trade-offs in multilingual settings.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train multilingual LLMs of varying sizes (100M to 10B parameters) on subsets of the following datasets: **XTREME-R**, **MasakhaNER**, and **CoNLL-2003** (English, German, and low-resource African languages). Baselines include **mBERT**, **XLM-R**, and **mT5**. Evaluate using perplexity, F1 score on named entity recognition tasks, and zero-shot cross-lingual transfer accuracy. Target >3% absolute F1 improvement on low-resource languages and achieve perplexity reductions below 15 on XTREME-R languages with <1M tokens. Experiments run on 8x A100 GPUs with 3 seeds per configuration; ablation studies will isolate effects of data diversity and size metrics.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws that treat multilingual data aggregates homogeneously, this work explicitly models linguistic diversity and data scarcity effects within a unified scaling framework, providing actionable formulas to optimize model and data allocation for equitable multilingual LLM performance—addressing a critical gap not covered by existing scaling law research or cross-lingual transfer studies.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a temporally-aware, dynamic capacity scaling framework for Large Language Models (LLMs) that optimally reallocates model capacity in response to evolving topical distributions in sequential social media corpora to maintain consistent perplexity and downstream task performance over time.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs assume static data distributions, ignoring temporal topical drift common in social media streams such as Reddit. This leads to suboptimal capacity allocation where models either overfit outdated topics or underfit emerging ones, causing degradation in perplexity and stability metrics over time. The open problem is how to incorporate explicit temporal non-stationarity and topical diversity into capacity scaling laws so that model size and training effort dynamically adapt to evolving data distributions.

APPROACH:  
We propose an adaptive model capacity scaling framework that integrates continual topic modeling with capacity allocation policies driven by temporal drift signals extracted from evolving corpora. By segmenting sequential datasets (e.g., monthly Reddit comments) and annotating topical shifts via dynamic topic models, capacity is selectively increased or pruned for topic-specific submodules within the LLM architecture. This method combines a temporal topic-aware regularizer with a capacity reallocation algorithm that updates scaling laws as a function of topic novelty, diversity, and frequency trends, effectively guiding dynamic model expansion or compression over time.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on sequential Reddit comment datasets with topic annotations (Pushshift Reddit Corpus with dynamic LDA topic models), C4 web text snapshots from different years, and Twitter streaming data with hashtag-based topic clusters. Baselines include GPT-2 (medium), GPT-3 (175B), and a static capacity variant of the proposed method. Metrics: perplexity over time (target <20 on Reddit monthly splits), topic-coherence stability (>0.75 normalized PMI), and downstream zero-shot classification accuracy on topical sentiment tasks with >3% absolute improvement over baselines. Experimental protocol involves training on 12 months of data sequentially on NVIDIA A100 GPUs (8 GPUs, 1.5 months total), with 3 seeds per setting and ablations on capacity reallocation frequency and topic granularity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior scaling laws which assume stationary data distributions and static model capacity (e.g., Kaplan et al. 2020, or compute-optimal scaling laws), this work explicitly models temporal topical drift and content diversity to dynamically adjust capacity allocation within the LLM. Previous work does not leverage temporal topic signals for capacity scaling, nor validates sustained performance on temporally evolving corpora, making this the first framework to produce time-aware scaling laws that improve model robustness and efficiency in non-stationary real-world data streams.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a principled, multi-objective constrained optimization framework that dynamically adapts large language model (LLM) pretraining dataset composition in real-time to maximize scaling efficiency by balancing data source quality, diversity, domain relevance, and noise levels across training stages.

BACKGROUND:  
Current LLM scaling approaches rely on fixed or heuristically mixed datasets, which fail to explicitly optimize the trade-offs between dataset size, quality, and domain diversity as model scale increases. This static composition limits the ability to fully exploit data-driven scaling laws for better generalization and downstream performance. The open problem is how to systematically and dynamically optimize dataset mixture ratios during training to improve scaling behavior and robustness across diverse tasks.

APPROACH:  
We formulate dataset composition as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem that jointly maximizes expected scaling gains and domain coverage while minimizing noise and domain bias. Using a feedback loop informed by intermediate evaluation on multiple validation sets (e.g., SuperGLUE, WinoGrande), the method adaptively adjusts the sampling proportions of curated corpora (like The Pile, C4) and raw web data (e.g., Common Crawl) throughout training. The optimization leverages differentiable proxy metrics for data quality and diversity, combined with resource constraints (compute and data budget), enabling a principled scheduling of dataset mixtures optimized for scaling laws and downstream generalization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train transformer-based LLMs at three scales (100M, 1B, 10B parameters) on combined datasets: The Pile, C4, and Common Crawl filtered subsets. Evaluate zero-shot and few-shot performance on benchmarks SuperGLUE, WinoGrande, and LAMBADA using exact match, accuracy, and perplexity metrics. Aim to achieve >3% absolute accuracy improvement on SuperGLUE over fixed-mixing baselines (e.g., uniform or heuristic mixing) and reduce perplexity by at least 10% on LAMBADA at equivalent compute. Conduct ablation studies on the impact of noise constraint thresholds and domain diversity weights, with at least 3 random seeds per condition run on A100 GPUs within a fixed 5M token compute budget.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike Balanced Dataset Composition Optimization for Open-Domain Scaling, which optimizes static dataset mixtures before training, our approach dynamically adapts dataset composition during training via a closed-loop constrained multi-objective optimization informed by intermediate task performance. Prior scaling laws focus on model size and data quantity, but our method explicitly integrates real-time dataset quality and domain diversity trade-offs, enabling more efficient scaling and better generalization that static heuristics cannot achieve.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, on-device incremental protein folding refinement framework that combines coarse-grained initial predictions with adaptive local structural updates guided by uncertainty-aware neural modules, enabling real-time, streaming improvement of protein models on resource-constrained edge hardware.

BACKGROUND: Current state-of-the-art protein structure predictors like AlphaFold2 deliver high-accuracy predictions but require massive computational resources, restricting their use to offline high-performance servers. Existing incremental refinement approaches generate coarse initial folds and improve them iteratively, yet they lack integrated uncertainty estimation and adaptive resource allocation, limiting their effectiveness for real-time, interactive experimental feedback on edge devices with strict compute and memory budgets.

APPROACH: We propose a novel modular pipeline that produces an initial coarse protein fold on-device using a simplified graph neural network, followed by iterative local refinement steps driven by an uncertainty-aware lightweight neural module that identifies and targets the most structurally ambiguous regions. Each refinement update applies minimal local conformational adjustments via a parameter-efficient equivariant message-passing network, bounded by strict per-update compute and latency budgets. The method exploits adaptive iteration control based on real-time confidence metrics to optimize resource usage during streaming prediction, enabling progressive accuracy improvements without exceeding edge device constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the CASP13 and CASP14 test sets, as well as the CAMEO continuous evaluation benchmark, comparing against baselines AlphaFold2, OpenFold, and the original On-Device Incremental Folding Refinement method. Metrics will include TM-score and RMSD improvements per iteration, cumulative accuracy gain after fixed iteration budgets, and end-to-end latency measured on representative edge hardware (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier). Ablations will examine the contribution of uncertainty-guided refinement and adaptive iteration control. Target improvements include >5% cumulative TM-score increase over baseline incremental methods within fixed latency budgets and latency reductions of at least 30% per refinement step relative to monolithic models.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior monolithic or incremental folding models, our method uniquely integrates real-time uncertainty estimation with adaptive, confidence-driven local refinement, enabling bounded compute, progressive accuracy improvement, and practical deployment on edge devices—capabilities absent from existing AlphaFold-based or incremental approaches that require large compute or lack streaming adaptability.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Active learning-driven generative modeling conditioned on multi-modal feedback integrates AlphaFold-derived structural priors with dynamic binding affinity and conformational uncertainty estimates to efficiently explore protein conformational landscapes beyond static predictions.

BACKGROUND:  
AlphaFold2 and related models predict static, high-confidence protein structures but lack practical mechanisms to explore protein conformational flexibility critical for drug binding, allostery, and induced fit. Current generative approaches for sampling protein conformations do not leverage iterative feedback loops combining structural predictions, binding affinity proxies, and model uncertainty, resulting in inefficient exploration of biologically relevant conformations. This gap limits the discovery of functional states and ligand-bound conformers needed for drug design.

APPROACH:  
We propose an active learning pipeline that integrates AlphaFold2-generated structural priors with a generative model (e.g., a graph-based VAE or equivariant normalizing flow) conditioned on multi-modal feedback: (1) uncertainty quantification from ensemble AlphaFold predictions and Bayesian neural network affinity predictors trained on binding data, and (2) predicted binding affinity scores from learned scoring functions (e.g., pretrained ChemBERTa-based affinity models fine-tuned on ChEMBL). The generative model iteratively refines sampling by focusing on conformations with high predicted binding potential and high model uncertainty, forming a closed-loop system that prioritizes exploration of the most promising conformational subspaces. This method conditions sampling on structure-function relationships beyond static folds by combining protein backbone flexibility, side-chain rearrangements, and ligand-induced conformational changes.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on the Protein Data Bank (PDBbind v2020), ChEMBL 29 ligand-target pairs with known binding conformations, and the CASP14 test set. Baselines include vanilla AlphaFold2 structural prediction, DeepFragLib fragment assembly, and unguided generative models such as GraphAF and GeoDiff. Metrics will include: (1) sampling efficiency measured by percentage of generated conformations within 2Å RMSD of known binding poses (>15% improvement expected), (2) hit rate improvement in high-affinity conformation discovery (>20% increase over unguided baselines), and (3) reduction in total compute time to reach convergence (>30% decrease). Ablation studies will isolate the effects of uncertainty-driven sampling, multi-modal conditioning, and AlphaFold priors. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~50 GPU days and repeated with 5 random seeds for statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as DeepTracer-LowResEnhance and standard AlphaFold2 applications, this method explicitly integrates iterative active learning based on both structural uncertainty and binding affinity feedback into generative sampling, enabling efficient exploration of drug-relevant dynamic conformations rather than static folds. It closes the loop between generative modeling and predictive scoring, a capability absent from current AlphaFold-based or unguided generative sampling approaches.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-resolution hierarchical multi-modal graph neural network (HR-MM-GNN) framework that integrates AlphaFold-derived monomer embeddings, sparse experimental crosslinking mass spectrometry (XL-MS), and low-resolution cryo-EM density maps to predict accurate quaternary structures of very large multi-protein complexes exceeding 1 MDa, enabling scalable joint modeling of combinatorial subunit interactions with uncertainty-aware data fusion.

BACKGROUND:  
Current methods, including the tournament-winning Hierarchical Multi-Modal GNN, struggle to scale to extremely large multi-protein complexes due to combinatorial explosion and inability to effectively integrate heterogeneous, sparse experimental modalities with high-confidence monomeric predictions from AlphaFold. The open problem is to develop a scalable, data-efficient architecture that fuses structural priors from AlphaFold with experimentally sparse and low-resolution data to accurately reconstruct spatial protein assemblies beyond the size and resolution limits of existing approaches.

APPROACH:  
We propose HR-MM-GNN, which first embeds individual proteins using AlphaFold-generated structural and sequence embeddings, then models inter-subunit interfaces via a hierarchical graph where nodes represent protein domains and edges encode potential contacts informed by XL-MS constraints and cryo-EM density correlations. We incorporate an uncertainty-aware fusion module that weighs contributions from AlphaFold confidence metrics, XL-MS distance restraints, and cryo-EM voxel intensity maps at multiple spatial resolutions. The architecture employs iterative message passing with domain-level coarse graphs refined into residue-level fine graphs within detected interface regions, enabling tractable modeling of thousands of subunits. Training uses contrastive loss on interface RMSD and assembly accuracy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the ComplexNet benchmark, the EMPIAR cryo-EM dataset of large assemblies, and the Protein-Protein Docking Benchmark Version 5. Metrics include interface RMSD (target <5Å), complex assembly accuracy (>85% correct inter-subunit contacts), and precision-recall on detected interfaces. Baselines for comparison comprise the original Hierarchical Multi-Modal GNN, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and AlphaFold-Multimer v2. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 1000 GPU hours, performing 5 independent runs for statistical significance and ablations on modality contributions and hierarchical depth.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods, HR-MM-GNN uniquely integrates AlphaFold monomer embeddings directly with multi-resolution hierarchical graphs and explicitly models uncertainty in data fusion across sparse XL-MS and low-res cryo-EM maps, enabling scalable and accurate prediction of very large multi-protein complexes (>1 MDa), a scale and integration that previous single-modal or flat graph approaches cannot handle effectively.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid deep learning and physics-based refinement framework that integrates AlphaFold-predicted subunit structures with low-resolution (5-10 Å) cryo-EM density maps to enable accurate, biologically realistic modeling of entire large macromolecular assemblies by explicitly optimizing fit-to-map and inter-subunit interface energetics.

BACKGROUND:  
Current AI-driven protein structure predictors, including AlphaFold2, excel at monomeric or small complex predictions but fail to reliably model large macromolecular complexes where only low-resolution cryo-EM maps (~5-10 Å) are available. The open problem is the inability to refine predicted subunit structures and assemble them into higher-order complexes that fit ambiguous density maps without introducing steric clashes or unrealistic conformations. Existing methods cannot jointly optimize structural fidelity to noisy, sparse experimental data and biologically plausible inter-subunit arrangements, limiting mechanistic insights into cellular machinery.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-stage pipeline combining (1) AlphaFold2-multimer predicted subunit models, (2) a novel differentiable density fitting module that maximizes correlation with low-resolution cryo-EM maps, and (3) a physics-informed energy-based refinement step using molecular mechanics force fields enhanced by graph neural network potentials to optimize interfacial contacts and global assembly geometry. The system employs a loss function jointly balancing map fit, stereochemical quality, and interface energetics, optimized via gradient-based methods. Additionally, a learned confidence reweighting module dynamically modulates contributions from predicted flexible regions and ambiguous density areas to avoid overfitting noise.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on publicly available benchmark datasets with experimentally determined low-resolution cryo-EM maps and validated complex structures: (1) EMDB Challenge datasets (e.g., EMDB-3344, EMDB-8753), (2) CASP15 Cryo-EM target set, and (3) the Protein Data Bank's large assembly subset (>300 kDa complexes). Baselines include AlphaFold2-multimer, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance (2024), and Rosetta cryo-EM refinement. Quantitative metrics: global map-model Fourier Shell Correlation (FSC) improvement by ≥5%, interface RMSD reduction by ≥1.5 Å, and MolProbity score improvement ≥0.5 compared to baselines. Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~1000 GPU-hours, running 5 independent seeds per target, including ablations testing impact of physics-based refinement and confidence reweighting.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike DeepTracer-LowResEnhance and related methods which focus primarily on map enhancement or single-chain fitting, our approach uniquely combines explicit physics-based interface optimization with differentiable map fitting in an end-to-end manner, enabling accurate assembly-level modeling in low-resolution contexts. Prior methods cannot jointly refine predicted subunits into large assemblies preserving biologically plausible interfaces while accommodating noisy experimental density; our framework overcomes this core limitation.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a catalytic mechanism-aware generative transformer model that jointly predicts protein backbone folds and sidechain conformations optimized for stable, novel enzyme active sites, explicitly integrating quantum mechanics-derived catalytic constraints and reaction pathway-informed loss functions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative protein design methods, including the winning approach “Catalytic site-aware generative modeling for novel synthetic enzyme fold design,” still struggle to reliably produce enzyme folds with catalytically competent and stable active sites, largely because they do not fully incorporate detailed reaction mechanism constraints or sidechain-level accuracy during fold generation. While AlphaFold and related systems excel at fold prediction, they lack catalytic site design awareness. Thus, designing entirely novel enzyme folds with stable, functionally valid active sites for specific biochemical reactions remains an unmet challenge.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel generative transformer architecture that integrates: (1) backbone fold generation conditioned on catalytic site geometry constraints extracted from quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) simulations of target reaction mechanisms; (2) joint sidechain conformation prediction optimizing both fold stability and catalytic residue positioning using differentiable reaction pathway-aware loss functions; and (3) a multi-task training regime combining structural prediction with enzymatic activity surrogate modeling based on physics-informed energy terms. This pipeline directly incorporates catalytic pocket stability and reaction transition state stabilization metrics within fold and sidechain generation, enabling design of enzyme folds with rare conformations highly optimized for functional catalysis.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on experimentally validated enzyme structure and activity datasets: (1) the Catalytic Site Atlas (CSA) dataset for known enzyme active site annotation; (2) the De Novo Enzyme Design dataset from the Baker Lab containing experimentally characterized synthetic enzymes; (3) the Protein Data Bank (PDB) subset of high-resolution enzyme structures with QM/MM-calculated reaction profiles. Baselines include: (a) the original Catalytic Site-Aware Generative Model (CSAGM), (b) AlphaFold2 as a structure predictor baseline, and (c) Rosetta-based enzyme design protocols. Metrics to evaluate: (i) catalytic site RMSD (target <1.5 Å improvement over CSAGM), (ii) fold novelty measured by TM-score (<0.5 vs known folds, indicating novel folds), (iii) predicted enzymatic activity via physics-based free energy of activation estimates (target >10% improvement over baseline predictions), and (iv) stability scores such as Rosetta ΔΔG and AlphaFold’s pLDDT confidence. Experiments will be trained on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for up to 2 weeks, with five random seeds for reproducibility and ablation studies isolating the impact of QM-informed loss and joint sidechain modeling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative models that only loosely incorporate catalytic pocket constraints or treat sidechain prediction as a post hoc step, this method tightly integrates reaction mechanism data from QM/MM simulations directly into the training objectives and jointly models backbone and sidechain conformations. This end-to-end catalytic mechanism-aware design enables generation of enzyme folds with experimentally unprecedented active site stability and functional novelty—capabilities not achievable by AlphaFold, CSAGM, or standard Rosetta enzyme design methods.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an ultra-low power neuromorphic co-design framework that synergistically integrates algorithmic temporal sparsity with mixed-signal event-driven hardware using adaptive on-chip memory hierarchies to achieve sub-microsecond latency and >10× energy efficiency improvements for real-time Edge AI tasks.

BACKGROUND:  
Edge AI devices face stringent constraints on power, latency, and memory, yet must respond immediately to asynchronous sensory inputs. Current neuromorphic approaches fail to jointly optimize these factors due to fixed memory architectures and inability to dynamically balance computational throughput with event-driven sparsity, resulting in suboptimal energy consumption and delayed responsiveness in real-world edge scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a co-design methodology combining (1) a novel adaptive temporal sparsity algorithm that dynamically adjusts spiking activity based on input event rates and task criticality, (2) a mixed-signal neuromorphic hardware architecture featuring hierarchical on-chip memory with fast local SRAM caches coupled with ultra-low leakage non-volatile memory for synaptic weights, and (3) event-driven asynchronous circuits that enable fine-grained power gating and prioritized processing of high-importance sensory events. This synergy maximizes energy efficiency while ensuring real-time processing under tight hardware constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic vision datasets N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and CIFAR10-DVS using the baselines Spiking ResNet (2016), the ReS2/h-BN/Graphene opto-electronic synapse model (2025), and the ferroelectric synaptic transistor DropConnect implementation (2025). Metrics include:  
- Energy per inference (target <10 pJ/inference, >10× reduction vs baselines)  
- End-to-end latency (target <1 ms, 30% lower than baselines)  
- Classification accuracy (maintain within 1% absolute of baseline)  
Experiments run on FPGA prototype and simulated mixed-signal ASIC with 5 seeds per setup; ablation studies on memory hierarchy depth, sparsity levels, and event prioritization thresholds.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats algorithm and hardware optimizations separately or relies on fixed memory designs, this approach uniquely co-optimizes temporal sparsity with adaptive hierarchical memory and fine-grained event-driven hardware, enabling orders-of-magnitude energy savings and latency reductions simultaneously—capabilities that neither fixed-sparsity algorithms nor purely device-level synaptic innovations can achieve alone.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a memory-constrained analog in-memory neural network compression framework that jointly optimizes structured pruning, analog-device-aware quantization, and cross-layer weight sharing to reduce on-chip memory by >50% with <1% accuracy loss, validated on large-scale neuromorphic benchmarks beyond MNIST.

BACKGROUND:  
Current analog in-memory computing frameworks inadequately address the severe memory constraints imposed by analog device non-idealities and storage limitations, often resulting in large circuit footprints or unacceptable accuracy degradation. Existing methods, such as pruning and quantization, are not jointly optimized to leverage analog device characteristics, nor do they explore advanced compression like cross-layer weight sharing, limiting scalability to complex datasets. This gap prevents practical deployment of energy-efficient neuromorphic computing on memory-constrained analog hardware.

APPROACH:  
We propose a unified compression pipeline that incorporates (1) structured pruning guided by analog hardware error models to remove redundant weights while preserving analog inference fidelity, (2) device-aware mixed-precision quantization tailored to the nonlinearities and noise profiles of analog synaptic elements, and (3) cross-layer weight sharing exploiting analog memory retention capabilities to minimize unique stored parameters. This pipeline is integrated into a hardware-in-the-loop training framework that simulates realistic analog device behaviors during model compression and fine-tuning to ensure robust accuracy. The framework is tested on complex neuromorphic benchmarks such as N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and CIFAR-10 converted into spiking formats.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation is performed on N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and spiking CIFAR-10 datasets using Intel Loihi and BrainScaleS neuromorphic platforms as hardware baselines. Compression baselines include: (a) Memory-Constrained Analog In-memory Neural Network Compression (the tournament winner method), (b) Deep Compression with Pruning + Quantization, and (c) Hardware-Implemented DropConnect Function. Metrics include memory footprint reduction (>50% target vs baseline), inference accuracy (≥95% on N-MNIST, ≥90% on DVS Gesture, ≥85% on spiking CIFAR-10), and energy per inference (target ≥30% reduction vs tournament winner method). Experiments involve five independent runs with different initialization seeds and ablation studies isolating each compression component.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work—which applies pruning and quantization separately without analog-device-tailored joint optimization—this method uniquely combines structured pruning, analog-aware mixed-precision quantization, and cross-layer weight sharing within a hardware-in-the-loop framework to achieve significantly greater memory compression (>50%) without sacrificing accuracy. This enables practical scaling of analog neuromorphic systems to larger, more complex spiking datasets and hardware beyond MNIST benchmarks.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop a co-designed adaptive event-driven neuromorphic architecture leveraging dynamic sparsity control and context-aware power gating to achieve ultra-low power, real-time processing tailored for noisy, sparse event streams in edge IoT devices.

BACKGROUND: Edge IoT devices face an open problem of efficiently processing sparse, noisy event-driven sensor data with minimal latency and energy consumption under severe resource constraints and intermittent connectivity. Existing neuromorphic approaches, including optoelectronic synapses and hardware stochastic DropConnect, lack adaptive mechanisms to dynamically tune computation based on event sparsity and context, resulting in wasted energy or degraded performance in unpredictable edge environments.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive event-driven neuromorphic computing framework combining (1) a novel dynamic sparsity controller that modulates synaptic activity and neuron firing thresholds in real time based on input event statistics, and (2) hardware-level context-aware power gating that selectively disables idle neurosynaptic cores to minimize leakage power. This is implemented via a hardware-software co-design integrating low-power event-driven spiking neural networks with a lightweight runtime optimizer that exploits temporal and spatial sparsity patterns in datasets, enabling robust, energy-proportional computation tailored to edge IoT signals.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the DVS128 Gesture Dataset, N-TIDIGITS (neuromorphic speech), and CIFAR10-DVS, benchmarking against TrueNorth, Loihi, and the Adaptive Event-Driven Computation framework baseline. Metrics: reduce average energy per inference by ≥40% versus baseline, achieve latency under 10 ms on edge-class hardware, and improve event-processing F1 score by at least 3% absolute. Experiments include 5 independent runs per dataset, ablation of sparsity controller and power gating modules, and hardware energy profiling on FPGA emulation with a fixed 4W compute budget.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either focuses on static or hardware-only energy reductions (e.g., ferroelectric synapses, 2D-material neurotransistors) or optoelectronic synapses lacking adaptive event tuning, this approach uniquely combines adaptive dynamic sparsity control with context-aware power gating in a co-designed neuromorphic system, enabling real-time, robust, and significantly more energy-efficient computation for unpredictable, sparse event streams typical of edge IoT scenarios.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an integrated low-power neuromorphic system combining optimized bio-inspired spiking plasticity algorithms with novel mixed-signal hardware featuring adaptive noise-resilient synaptic updates, enabling real-time, energy-efficient on-chip learning for edge IoT devices under highly variable sensory input conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Edge IoT devices require autonomous, continual learning capabilities that operate within stringent energy and area constraints while handling noisy and sparse event-driven sensory data. Current neuromorphic approaches suffer from excessive energy consumption during on-chip learning phases and show poor robustness to environmental noise, limiting practical deployment. Specifically, there is no existing integrated solution that jointly optimizes spiking plasticity algorithms and hardware architectures for low-power, noise-robust, and rapid adaptation on real-world sensory streams.

APPROACH:  
We propose a co-designed system that integrates a novel bio-inspired spiking plasticity learning rule extending classical STDP with adaptive noise filtering and dynamic synaptic scaling, implemented on a mixed-signal neuromorphic chip featuring low-leakage ferroelectric synaptic transistors and embedded stochastic DropConnect-like dropout hardware. The system dynamically modulates synaptic weight update precision based on input noise characteristics, reducing energy overhead by up to 40% during learning phases. Additionally, the hardware supports in-situ calibration enabling robustness to sparse and noisy event streams typical in edge environments, facilitating rapid on-chip adaptation with minimal computational overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic sensory datasets including N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and the SHD (Spiking Heidelberg Digits) dataset, benchmarking against baselines such as SLAYER, Loihi’s on-chip learning, and the ferroelectric DropConnect synaptic transistor approach from “Hardware-Implemented DropConnect Function for Energy-Efficient Neuromorphic Computing.” Metrics include classification accuracy (>2% absolute improvement over baselines), energy consumption per inference and per learning update (targeting ≥30% reduction vs Loihi), on-chip learning latency (reduce by ≥25%), and robustness to additive noise (maintain >90% accuracy with up to 20% event dropout). Experiments will run on a custom FPGA prototype and silicon test chip over 5 random seeds with systematic ablations on noise filtering and synaptic scaling modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that separately addresses energy efficiency in synaptic devices or algorithmic plasticity, this research uniquely fuses adaptive noise-aware spiking plasticity with mixed-signal hardware featuring stochastic synaptic dropout for on-chip learning, achieving simultaneous improvements in energy efficiency, noise robustness, and learning speed. This integrated approach surpasses existing neuromorphic systems, which either lack practical noise resilience or impose high energy overheads during real-time adaptation in edge scenarios.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a probabilistic error-compensation and adaptive selective re-computation framework that tightly integrates approximate in-memory neuromorphic computing with dynamic error bounding to achieve >35% energy savings while maintaining >98% service-level accuracy for high-throughput, reliable neuromorphic services in data center environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current in-memory neuromorphic computing systems aggressively apply approximate computing to reduce energy consumption but suffer from unbounded error propagation that degrades service-level accuracy, making them unsuitable for reliable, large-scale deployment in data centers. Existing solutions lack a principled mechanism to integrate approximate results into complex multi-stage neuromorphic pipelines without violating strict service-level agreements (SLAs), limiting throughput and energy efficiency.

APPROACH:  
We propose an integrated framework combining probabilistic error modeling that quantifies error propagation across neuromorphic layers with an adaptive selective re-computation mechanism triggered by service-level accuracy monitors. The system dynamically bounds cumulative errors by selectively re-computing critical synaptic or neuron states only when probabilistic confidence metrics predict SLA violations, thus maximizing approximation benefits while ensuring reliability. Implementation includes in-memory approximate computing modules coupled with lightweight online error estimators embedded in neuromorphic server pipelines.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic benchmarks including N-MNIST (event-based vision), DVS Gesture dataset (dynamic vision sensing), and IBM TrueNorth's NSL-KDD (network intrusion detection). Baselines for comparison include the Error-Resilient Approximate Computing Integration framework (tournament winner), DropConnect Hardware-Implemented Ferroelectric Synaptic Transistor (2025), and ReS2/h-BN/Graphene Opto-electronic Synapses (2025). Metrics assessed are service-level agreement accuracy (target >98%), energy savings (target >35% reduction vs baseline), and throughput improvements (target >20% increase). Experimental protocol involves training on dedicated neuromorphic hardware simulators with FPGA-accelerated in-memory computing emulators, three independent runs with different seeds, and detailed ablation studies on error threshold parameters and re-computation strategies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either applies static approximate computing or hardware-level stochastic techniques without integrated error propagation control, this approach introduces a probabilistic error modeling layer combined with adaptive re-computation that proactively bounds error growth within complex neuromorphic service pipelines. This enables reliable, high-throughput neuromorphic computing for demanding data center applications while maximizing energy efficiency beyond current state-of-the-art limits.
